tensor_equation: ∀program P: (¬∃data_race(P)) ⇒ (SC(P))
section: go.dev/ref/mem#introduction — DRF-SC Guarantee
counterexample: A program with a data race (e.g., one goroutine writes int x while another reads x without synchronization) has undefined behavior and is NOT guaranteed sequentially consistent execution.

tensor_equation: ∀goroutine g: ∀operations op1, op2 in g: (sequenced_before(op1, op2) ∧ op1 visible_to op2) ⇒ consistent_sequential_execution(g)
section: go.dev/ref/mem#introduction — Operation Classification
counterexample: A goroutine that reads a value that was never written to that location (out-of-thin-air) would violate this — but compilers are forbidden from producing such reads.

tensor_equation: ∀ordinary_read r at location x: r ≠ synchronizing ∧ ∃write w at x: (happens_before(w, r) ∧ ¬∃write w' at x: (happens_before(w, w') ∧ happens_before(w', r))) ⇒ W(r) = w
section: go.dev/ref/mem#happens-before — Requirement 3: Ordinary Data Read Visibility
counterexample: var x int; goroutine A: x = 1; goroutine B: print(x) — if B observes x==1 but A's write was supposed to happen after B's read according to happens-before, that's a violation (but actually just a data race).

tensor_equation: ∃read_like r, write_like w at location x: (¬happens_before(r, w) ∧ ¬happens_before(w, r) ∧ (¬is_synchronizing(r) ∨ ¬is_synchronizing(w))) ⇒ data_race(r, w)
section: go.dev/ref/mem#happens-before — Data Race Formal Definition (Read-Write)
counterexample: var x int; goroutine A: x = 1; goroutine B: println(x) — no synchronization, so x=1 and println(x) are unordered by happens-before → read-write data race.

tensor_equation: ∃write_like w1, write_like w2 at location x: (w1 ≠ w2 ∧ ¬happens_before(w1, w2) ∧ ¬happens_before(w2, w1) ∧ (¬is_synchronizing(w1) ∨ ¬is_synchronizing(w2))) ⇒ data_race(w1, w2)
section: go.dev/ref/mem#happens-before — Data Race Formal Definition (Write-Write)
counterexample: var x int; goroutine A: x = 1; goroutine B: x = 2 — two writes to x with no synchronization → write-write data race.

tensor_equation: ∀package p, q: (imports(p, q)) ⇒ (happens_before(completion(q.init), start(p.init)))
section: go.dev/ref/mem#inititialization — HB1: Package initialization ordering
counterexample: package a imports package b. If b.init writes to a global and a.init reads it after b.init completes, the read is guaranteed to see the write. No counterexample possible in compliant Go.

tensor_equation: ∀package p: happens_before(completion(p.init), start(p.main))
section: go.dev/ref/mem#inititialization — HB2: All init complete before main.main
counterexample: If main.main() accesses a global set by an init function in another package, it is guaranteed to see the initialized value. No counterexample in compliant Go.

tensor_equation: ∀goroutine g started by go_stmt S: ∃hb_edge: happens_before(evaluation(S), start(g))
section: go.dev/ref/mem#goroutine-creation — HB3: Go statement before goroutine start
counterexample: var a string; func f() { print(a) }; func hello() { a = "hello, world"; go f() } — the write to a happens-before the go statement, which happens-before f executes, so f must see a == "hello, world".

tensor_equation: ¬∃event e: happens_before(exit(g), e) for goroutine g
section: go.dev/ref/mem#goroutine-destruction — HB4: Goroutine exit NOT synchronized
counterexample: var a string; func hello() { go func() { a = "hello" }(); print(a) } — the write to a by the goroutine is NOT guaranteed to be observed by print(a). The goroutine's exit provides no happens-before edge.

tensor_equation: ∀channel c: (send(c, val) ∧ receive(c) returns val) ⇒ happens_before(send(c, val), completion(receive(c)))
section: go.dev/ref/mem#channel-communication — HB6: Channel send happens-before corresponding receive
counterexample: var c = make(chan int, 10); var a string; func f() { a = "hello, world"; c <- 0 }; func main() { go f(); <-c; print(a) } — without the channel, print(a) might see empty string. With it, the send happens-before the receive, so a is guaranteed initialized.

tensor_equation: ∀channel c: happens_before(close(c), receive(c) that returns zero due to closed_channel)
section: go.dev/ref/mem#channel-communication — HB7: Channel close happens-before zero-value receive
counterexample: Replace c <- 0 with close(c) in GOMEM-010's example — same guarantee: close(c) happens-before the receive that returns zero, so writes before close are visible.

tensor_equation: ∀unbuffered_channel c: happens_before(receive(c), completion(corresponding_send(c)))
section: go.dev/ref/mem#channel-communication — HB8: Unbuffered channel receive before send completion
counterexample: var c = make(chan int); var a string; func f() { a = "hello, world"; <-c }; func main() { go f(); c <- 0; print(a) } — on unbuffered channel, receive happens-before send completes, so the write to a in f happens-before the send returns, guaranteeing print(a) sees "hello, world".

tensor_equation: ∀buffered_channel c with capacity C, ∀k ≥ 0: happens_before(kth_receive(c), completion((k+C)th_send(c)))
section: go.dev/ref/mem#channel-communication — HB9: kth receive before (k+C)th send on buffered channel
counterexample: With make(chan int, 3), send #3 happens-before receive #1 returns. To prove: the kth receive takes a value placed by send #(k) or earlier, freeing a slot. Send #(k+3) blocks until that slot is freed, so the kth receive finishes before send #(k+3) writes.

tensor_equation: ∀mutex l, ∀n < m: happens_before(completion(nth Unlock(l)), return of mth Lock(l))
section: go.dev/ref/mem#locks — HB10: Mutex Unlock#n happens-before Lock#(n+1)
counterexample: var l sync.Mutex; var a string; func f() { a = "hello, world"; l.Unlock() }; func main() { l.Lock(); go f(); l.Lock(); print(a) } — Lock#2 happens after Unlock#1, so a is guaranteed initialized: prints "hello, world".

tensor_equation: ∀rwmutex l, ∀n: (happens_before(completion(nth Unlock(l)), return of RLock(l) that acquires after nth Unlock) ∧ happens_before(completion(RUnlock(l)), return of (n+1)th Lock(l)))
section: go.dev/ref/mem#locks — HB11: RWMutex RLock/RUnlock ordering
counterexample: var l sync.RWMutex; var a string; func f() { l.RLock(); _ = a; l.RUnlock() } — without proper preceding Unlock#n establishing the happens-before edge from the writer, the read of a in the RLock section may observe stale data.

tensor_equation: ∀mutex l: (TryLock(l) returns true) ⇒ synchronization_equivalent(Lock(l)) ∧ (TryLock(l) returns false) ⇒ no_synchronization_effect
section: go.dev/ref/mem#locks — HB12: TryLock/TryRLock equivalence
counterexample: var l sync.Mutex; var x int; goroutine A: x = 1; l.Unlock() — goroutine B: if l.TryLock() { print(x) } — if TryLock succeeds, it provides the same happens-before edge as Lock() would, so x=1 is visible. If TryLock fails, no guarantee.

tensor_equation: ∀once sync.Once, ∀f func(): happens_before(completion(f() within once.Do(f)), return_of_all_calls_to_once.Do(f))
section: go.dev/ref/mem#once — HB13: Once.Do synchronizes the function completion to all callers
counterexample: var a string; var once sync.Once; setup() { a = "hello, world" }; doprint() { once.Do(setup); print(a) }; twoprint() { go doprint(); go doprint() } — both doprint calls see "hello, world" because once.Do(setup) completes setup's write to a before returning to any caller.

tensor_equation: ∀atomically_loaded_var x: (load(x, A) == store(x, B, val)) ⇒ happens_before(store(x, B, val), load(x, A))
section: go.dev/ref/mem#atomic-values — HB14: Atomic observation implies synchronization
counterexample: var x atomic.Int64; var s string; goroutine A: s = "hello"; x.Store(1) — goroutine B: if x.Load() == 1 { print(s) } — because Load observes Store's value, the Store happens-before the Load, so s is guaranteed initialized.

tensor_equation: ∀program P using sync/atomic: ∃sequentially_consistent_total_order T of all atomic_ops_in_P: T is consistent with sequencing_and_values
section: go.dev/ref/mem#atomic-values — HB15: Atomic operations are sequentially consistent
counterexample: var a, b atomic.Int64; goroutine A: a.Store(1); b.Store(1); goroutine B: if b.Load() == 1 { print(a.Load()) } — if B sees b==1, it must see a==1 because atomic ops execute in a sequentially consistent total order across all goroutines.

tensor_equation: ∀finalizer f, object x: happens_before(runtime.SetFinalizer(x, f), f(x))
section: go.dev/ref/mem#finalizers — HB16: SetFinalizer before finalization call
counterexample: var a string; type T struct{}; runtime.SetFinalizer(&T{}, func(t *T) { print(a) }) — if a is set after SetFinalizer but before GC runs, the finalizer is NOT guaranteed to see the later write to a, only writes that happened before SetFinalizer.

tensor_equation: ∀sync.WaitGroup wg: happens_before(wg.Add(N), goroutine that calls wg.Done()) ∧ happens_before(completion(Done()), return_of_wg.Wait())
section: go.dev/ref/mem#additional-mechanisms — HB17: WaitGroup synchronization via package documentation
counterexample: var wg sync.WaitGroup; var a string; wg.Add(1); go func() { a = "hello"; wg.Done() }(); wg.Wait(); print(a) — wg.Add(1) happens-before the goroutine starts; wg.Done() happens-before wg.Wait() returns; so print(a) must see "hello".

tensor_equation: ∀program P: P contains double_checked_locking ⇒ ¬∃hb_guarantee(writes_inside_conditional, reads_after_check)
section: go.dev/ref/mem#incorrect-synchronization — IS2: Double-checked locking is invalid
counterexample: var a string; var done bool; func setup() { a = "hello, world"; done = true }; func doprint() { if !done { once.Do(setup) }; print(a) } — even if done == true, the write to a is NOT guaranteed to be visible to doprint() because done is a plain (non-synchronizing) variable.

tensor_equation: ∀spin_loop L that reads non-synchronizing_data: (¬∃hb_edge from writer to reader(L)) ⇒ ¬guaranteed_termination(L)
section: go.dev/ref/mem#incorrect-synchronization — IS3: Busy-waiting without synchronization is invalid
counterexample: var a string; var done bool; func setup() { a = "hello"; done = true }; func main() { go setup(); for !done {}; print(a) } — the loop is NOT guaranteed to terminate because there is no synchronization between setup() and main(). The compiler may even delete the loop body entirely.

tensor_equation: ∀pointer p, field f: (load(p) == addr ∧ ¬∃sync between store(addr.f) and load(p)) ⇒ ¬hb_guarantee(store(addr.f), load(addr.f, deref(load(p))))
section: go.dev/ref/mem#incorrect-synchronization — IS4: Pointer observation does not guarantee content observation
counterexample: type T struct { msg string }; var g *T; func setup() { t := new(T); t.msg = "hello"; g = t }; func main() { go setup(); for g == nil {}; print(g.msg) } — even when main observes g != nil, g.msg may still be empty because there is no synchronization.

tensor_equation: ∀compiler C, program P: (¬∃write w in P) ⇒ (output(C(P)) must not contain w)
section: go.dev/ref/mem#incorrect-compilation — C1: Compiler must not introduce writes

tensor_equation: ∀compiler C, conditional branch B containing write w: C must not move w outside B unless the condition B provably holds
section: go.dev/ref/mem#incorrect-compilation — C4: Compiler must not move writes out of conditionals

tensor_equation: ∀compiler C: C must not move memory accesses across loop that might not terminate
section: go.dev/ref/mem#incorrect-compilation — C5: Compiler must not assume loops terminate
counterexample:
// n := 0; for e := list; e != nil; e = e.next { n++ }; i := *p
// INVALID to move *p before loop if list may be cyclic (infinite loop).

tensor_equation: ∀compiler C: C must not move memory accesses across function call unless C provably knows f always returns
section: go.dev/ref/mem#incorrect-compilation — C6: Compiler must not assume functions always return
counterexample:
// f(); i := *p
// INVALID to move *p before f() if f() may not return (infinite loop, panic, etc.).

tensor_equation: ∀compiler C: (C loads var i from *p, then discards i) ⇒ C must not reload i from *p; C must reuse the previously loaded value
section: go.dev/ref/mem#incorrect-compilation — C7: No reloading from shared memory after discard

tensor_equation: ∀compiler C, write w = *p = expr: C must not use *p as temporary storage before w completes
section: go.dev/ref/mem#incorrect-compilation — C8: No using destination memory as temporary storage

tensor_equation: ∀sizeof(T) > machine_word, ∀read r of T: implement(r) = read_individual_fields_in_any_order(T)
section: go.dev/ref/mem#implementation-restrictions-for-programs-containing-data-races — Composite type access order is unspecified
counterexample: type S struct { a int; b int }; goroutine A: s = S{1, 2}; goroutine B: x := s.a; y := s.b — if goroutine A writes concurrently, goroutine B may observe any interleaving of sub-value writes (a updated but not b, etc.). For word-sized reads the single-word rule applies.

tensor_equation: ∀read r of single-word location x: W(r) must be a write w such that ¬happens_before(r, w) ∧ ¬∃write w': happens_before(w, w') ∧ happens_before(w', r)
section: go.dev/ref/mem#implementation-restrictions-for-programs-containing-data-races — Single-word read observation
counterexample: var x int; goroutine A: x = 1; goroutine B: x = 2; goroutine C: print(x) — with no synchronization, C must observe either 1, 2, or the initial value (0). It must NOT observe some 'out-of-thin-air' value.

tensor_equation: ∀read r of multi-word location x: ¬(guarantee(r observes single_coherent_write)) — only encouraged, not required
section: go.dev/ref/mem#implementation-restrictions-for-programs-containing-data-races — Multi-word reads (performance exception)
counterexample: type P struct { ptr unsafe.Pointer; len int }; var p P; goroutine A: p = P{&buf, 100}; goroutine B: slice := p; _ = slice.ptr[slice.len-1] — if B observes len=100 but ptr still pointing at old buffer, this causes arbitrary memory corruption. For interface, map, slice, string values this is dangerous.

tensor_equation: ∀read r concurrent with write w on location x: (W(r) = w) ⇒ ¬∃hb_edge(w', r): happens_before(w', r) for any subsequent read r' that happens-after r but not-after w
section: go.dev/ref/mem#incorrect-synchronization — IS1: Concurrent read may observe concurrent write but seq-cst does NOT follow
counterexample: var a, b int; func f() { a = 1; b = 2 }; func g() { print(b); print(a) }; func main() { go f(); g() } — it is possible for g to print 2 then 0 (observes b = 2 from f but a = 0). Observing a concurrent write to b does NOT imply the write to a is visible.

tensor_equation: ∀cond *sync.Cond: happens_before(Broadcast/Signal(cond), return of Wait(cond) awakened by it)
section: go.dev/ref/mem#additional-mechanisms — HB17: sync.Cond synchronization
counterexample: var mu sync.Mutex; var cond = sync.NewCond(&mu); var ready bool; goroutine A: mu.Lock(); ready = true; cond.Broadcast(); mu.Unlock(); goroutine B: mu.Lock(); for !ready { cond.Wait() }; mu.Unlock() — Broadcast happens-before Wait returns, so ready=true is visible.

tensor_equation: ∀sync.Map m: happens_before(Store(m, k, v), Load(m, k) that returns v) when no concurrent mutation
section: go.dev/ref/mem#additional-mechanisms — HB17: sync.Map synchronization
counterexample: var m sync.Map; var a string; goroutine A: a = "hello"; m.Store("key", 1); goroutine B: m.Load("key"); print(a) — if B observes the stored value, the Store happens-before Load, and the write to a (which happened before Store) must be visible.
